title: Solo Marketer — Small Business
description: You're the only person doing marketing at a small Australian business. You might not even have 'Marketing' in your title — you could be 'Marketing & Communications Coordinator' or even 'Office Manager who also does marketing.' You do everything: social media, emails, website updates, flyers, events. It's scrappy, resourceful, and deeply rewarding when it works.

schedule.activity: Open the shop or office. Check Google reviews, social media comments, and email enquiries from overnight.
schedule.note: Customer service and marketing overlap heavily in small business

schedule.activity: Post today's social media content — photo from yesterday's event, customer testimonial, or product highlight
Quick chat with the owner about this week's priorities — new product arriving, upcoming sale, community event
Update the website with new products and this week's specials
Design a promotional flyer for the local market this weekend
Reply to customer DMs and comments on social media
Lunch — scroll through competitor socials and industry trends for inspiration
Write this week's email newsletter — feature a customer story and upcoming events
Take product photos for next week's social content
Review last month's sales data to understand what marketing drove results
Coordinate with a local influencer for a collaboration post
Update Google Business Profile with new photos and respond to reviews
Brainstorm next month's marketing initiatives with the owner
Print materials for this weekend's market stall, pack marketing collateral
Wrap up — queue tomorrow's social post, check email one last time

responsibilities: Manage all social media accounts (content creation, scheduling, engagement)
Create all marketing materials (digital and print)
Manage Google Business Profile and local SEO
Handle customer communication on social platforms
Coordinate local partnerships and community events
Track basic marketing metrics and report to the owner
challenges: Tiny budget (or no budget) — you need to be creative with free tools
Doing everything alone with no specialist support
No formal marketing training — learning on the job
Blurred lines between marketing, admin, and customer service
Difficulty measuring ROI when systems aren't sophisticated
The owner may not understand what marketing requires
rewards: Total creative freedom — you decide what to post, when, and how
Incredibly broad experience across every marketing channel
Direct relationship with customers — you see the human impact
Every skill you learn is immediately applicable
Perfect springboard to a bigger marketing role
careerPath: Marketing Manager at a medium business (2-4 years)
Start your own consultancy or agency
Transition to a specialist role (content, social, email) at a larger company
